plainMeaning: This verse simply calls out the names of more great souls to remember and admire: Halla and Vihalla, Sudarshana, Shala and Mahashala, Shalibhadra, Bhadra, Dasharnabhadra, Prasannachandra, and Yashobhadra.
recitation: halla vihalla sudaṁsaṇa, sāla mahāsāla sālibhaddo a; bhaddo dasaṇṇabhaddo, pasaṇṇacaṁdo a jasabhaddo.

wordByWord.gloss: Halla — a prince of Rajagriha, son of King Shrenika and Queen Chellana
wordByWord.etymology: Proper name; Prakrit Halla, stem in nominative without the usual -o ending (paired vocative-style with the next name).
wordByWord.notes: Brother of Vihalla; the pair possessed the divine elephant Sechanaka, the indirect cause of the Ratha-musala war with Kunika, after which both renounced under Mahavira.

wordByWord.gloss: Vihalla (Vehalla) — Halla's brother, who renounced the world with him
wordByWord.etymology: Proper name; Prakrit Vihalla / Vehalla, Sanskritized as Vehalla.
wordByWord.notes: Named as a pair with Halla; the two stand together as exemplars of abandoning royal wealth for the monastic path.

wordByWord.gloss: Sudarshana — the merchant of Champa, supreme male exemplar of śīla (chastity)
wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit su- ('good, fair') + darśana ('sight, appearance') → Sudarśana; Prakrit -daṁsaṇa with nasalized conjunct.
wordByWord.notes: Falsely accused, he was sentenced to impalement; by the power of his unbroken śīla the stake (śūlī) is said to have turned into a throne. He is the male counterpart to the satīs honoured later in the litany.

wordByWord.gloss: Shala — king of Prishthachampa who renounced under Mahavira
wordByWord.etymology: Proper name; Prakrit Sāla = Sanskrit Śāla.
wordByWord.notes: Named with his brother Mahashala; tradition holds both, with their nephew Gagali, attained kevalajñāna shortly after renouncing.

wordByWord.gloss: Mahashala — younger brother of Shala, who renounced alongside him
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit mahā- ('great') + Śāla → Mahāśāla; Prakrit Mahāsāla.
wordByWord.notes: The 'great Shala', paired with Shala as an example of brothers renouncing a kingdom together.

wordByWord.gloss: Shalibhadra — the immensely wealthy youth of Rajagriha who renounced all luxury
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit Śālibhadra (śāli, 'rice/prosperity' + bhadra, 'auspicious'); Prakrit -bhaddo, masculine nominative singular -o (= Skt -aḥ).
wordByWord.notes: Son of Bhadra Shethani; ninety-nine caskets of celestial goods are said to have descended for him daily. On learning the king was his 'master', he renounced, gave up his wives one by one, and ended in sallekhana on Vaibhara hill — the paragon of detachment from enjoyment (bhoga).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit a = Sanskrit ca ('and'), reduced by lenition of c- and loss of the vowel quality typical of Ardhamāgadhī.
wordByWord.notes: First of two occurrences; a metrical 'and' linking items in the roll-call, not adding meaning beyond enumeration.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhadra — taken in the tradition as Bhadrabāhu, the last śruta-kevalī
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bhadra ('auspicious, good'); Prakrit bhaddo with assimilated conjunct dr → dd and nominative -o.
wordByWord.notes: Identification is not certain: 'Bhaddo' is read by several commentators as Bhadrabāhu Svāmī (the last knower of the full Pūrva-śruta), though some recensions treat it as a distinct Bhadra-muni. Flagged as uncertain — see notes.

wordByWord.gloss: Dasharnabhadra — the king who conquered his pride before Mahavira and renounced
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit Daśārṇabhadra (Daśārṇa, his country, + bhadra); Prakrit dasaṇṇa- with rṇ → ṇṇ assimilation, + bhaddo.
wordByWord.notes: He resolved to honour Mahavira with unmatched pomp; when Indra (Śakra) arrived with far greater splendour, Dasharnabhadra realized the one glory Indra could not match was renunciation, and took diksha on the spot — the exemplar of conquering māna (pride).

wordByWord.gloss: Prasannachandra — the royal sage whose changing thoughts swung him between hell and omniscience
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit Prasannacandra (prasanna, 'serene/gracious' + candra, 'moon'); Prakrit pasaṇṇa- (pr → p, sn → ṇṇ) + caṁdo (candra → caṁdo).
wordByWord.notes: In kāyotsarga he mentally fought an imagined war and was momentarily fit for the seventh hell; recoiling in repentance he ascended the kṣapaka-śreṇi and reached kevalajñāna within moments — the classic proof that bhāva (mental state), not outward posture, binds and liberates.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit a = Sanskrit ca ('and'); same reduced enclitic as before.
wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence; again a purely enumerative 'and' joining the final name to the list.

wordByWord.gloss: Yashobhadra — the great ācārya of the early Śvetāmbara succession
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit Yaśobhadra (yaśas, 'fame' + bhadra); Prakrit jasa- (yaśas → jasa, y → j, ś → s) + bhaddo.
wordByWord.notes: Generally identified with Ācārya Yaśobhadrasūri, a teacher in the line leading to Sambhūtavijaya and Bhadrabāhu; honoured as a great śruta-dhara. Precise identification varies by recension — see notes.

literalTranslation: Halla, Vihalla, Sudarshana; Shala, Mahashala, and Shalibhadra; Bhadra, Dasharnabhadra; Prasannachandra, and Yashobhadra.
idiomaticTranslation: Remember Halla and Vihalla, and Sudarshana of unbroken purity; Shala and Mahashala, and Shalibhadra who let all his riches fall away; remember Bhadra, and Dasharnabhadra who laid down his pride; Prasannachandra, whose very thoughts won him omniscience, and Yashobhadra.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The third gāthā continues the roll-call of mahāsattvas, but the names are not chosen at random — each encodes a distinct conquest, so the reciter passes a small gallery of victories. Halla and Vihalla give up the elephant, the necklace and the throne itself: renunciation of inherited wealth and status. Sudarshana embodies śīla so unshakeable that even an unjust execution cannot stain it — the lone male standard-bearer of the chastity for which the satīs are praised later in the litany. Shala and Mahashala model brothers who leave a kingdom together and ripen swiftly to kevalajñāna. Shalibhadra, on whom heaven itself rained ninety-nine caskets daily, walks away from all of it the instant he learns he still has a 'master' — detachment from bhoga at its most vivid. Then the verse turns inward: Dasharnabhadra's victory is over māna, pride, achieving before Mahavira and Indra the one thing celestial power cannot — renunciation. Prasannachandra's is the most interior of all: standing motionless in kāyotsarga, his racing mind alone nearly earns him the seventh hell, and his repentant mind alone wins omniscience — bhāva is everything. The closing Bhadra and Yashobhadra anchor the list in the monastic śruta-lineage. Read as a set, the verse moves from renouncing outer wealth (Halla–Shalibhadra) to mastering inner faults (pride, then thought itself), quietly teaching that the harder battlefield is within.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse turns on the Jain doctrine that karma is bound chiefly by bhāva (intention/mental state), not outward act — nowhere clearer than Prasannachandra, whose stationary body conceals a mind swinging from hell-bound himsā to omniscience. Dasharnabhadra dramatizes the kaṣāya of māna and its dissolution; Sudarshana, the brahmacarya-vrata; Shalibhadra and the rest, aparigraha and vairāgya. Together they show the same path conquered along different fronts — outer renunciation and the inner victory over the four passions.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, these names invite a quick self-audit: Where am I a Dasharnabhadra clinging to pride? Am I, like Prasannachandra, sitting still in 'meditation' while my mind wages war? The verse reminds the reciter that the decisive spiritual work happens in the quality of one's thoughts, moment to moment.

sources.title: Shrenika (Bimbisara) — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Halla and Vihalla as sons of King Shrenika and Queen Chellana of Rajagriha, and the Magadha/Champa setting behind their renunciation.

sources.title: Ajatashatru (Kunika) — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Kunika's conflict with Halla and Vihalla over the divine elephant/necklace — the war that precipitated the brothers' turn to monkhood.

sources.title: Bhadrabāhu — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identification of 'Bhaddo' with Bhadrabāhu, the last śruta-kevalī, and his place in the early Śvetāmbara succession.

sources.title: Sthulabhadra — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: The early Śvetāmbara monastic lineage (Yaśobhadra–Sambhūtavijaya–Bhadrabāhu) used to place 'Jasabhaddo' (Yaśobhadra).

notes: Verse is a pure name-list of male exemplars (mahāsattvas); 100% of tokens glossed, including both occurrences of the connective 'a' (= Skt ca). Two identifications are uncertain and flagged: (1) 'Bhaddo' is most often read as Bhadrabāhu, the last śruta-kevalī, but some recensions take it as a distinct Bhadra-muni; (2) 'Jasabhaddo' is read as Ācārya Yaśobhadrasūri, though the exact figure varies across commentaries. Halla/Vihalla, Shala/Mahashala, Sudarshana, Shalibhadra, Dasharnabhadra and Prasannachandra are securely attested in the kathā tradition. Per the book's orthography the original is rendered verbatim; the recitation line is in IAST (target script Latin for English). General-purpose web search returned an unreliable answer treating these names as poetic 'metres' — that was discarded in favour of the canonical narrative tradition. Story details (e.g., the stake-turned-throne for Sudarshana, the seventh-hell moment for Prasannachandra) follow received tradition and may differ in detail across recensions.
